network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature-supported view that alanine is formed prebiotically through multiple convergent routes that predominantly generate racemic alanine or alanine precursors, and that L-alanine is most plausibly obtained only indirectly through symmetry breaking and amplification at the network level rather than by a single direct enantioselective synthesis step. The design therefore builds ten distinct pathways converging on alanine chemistry through the key hubs HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, alpha-aminopropionitrile, and DL-alanine, spanning hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical settings. The final step to L-alanine is represented as a coupled biochemical selection/amplification module grounded in the literature on CISS-enabled homochiral RNA precursors and stereoselective coded peptide synthesis, with explicit acknowledgment that this final conversion remains an open systems-level problem rather than a single established reaction.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 through iron-sulfur catalysis gives reduced one-carbon products including formaldehyde equivalents.
reactions.reasoning: Alkaline hydrothermal vents with Fe(Ni)S minerals and H2 are reported to generate formaldehyde from CO2/H2 under vent-like conditions, providing a hydrothermal feed into surface carbonyl chemistry (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate generation from CO2 via iron-promoted protometabolic carbon fixation

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni or Fe-based particles and mineral phases
conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal setting; network-level rTCA-like or nanoparticle-mediated CO2 fixation rather than a single elementary step

reactions.mechanism: Iron-promoted CO2 reduction and C-C bond-forming protometabolic chemistry generate pyruvate as a central alpha-keto acid node.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a literature-supported hydrothermal branch point formed in iron-promoted reverse-TCA-like chemistry and in CO2 reduction over Fe/Ni particles; representing this as a network condensation step avoids the unsupported overcompression criticized previously while remaining faithful to pyruvate as a documented prebiotic product (Muchowska, Varma, and Moran, 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Spark discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde from reduced gases

conditions.catalyst: mineral-rich shoreline condensate collecting electrical-discharge products
conditions.other: spark discharge atmosphere, one-week cycling, reduced gas mixture with condensation into aqueous pools

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced atmospheres produces nitriles and aldehydes in the gas phase, which dissolve into surface waters where further Strecker chemistry proceeds.
reactions.reasoning: Miller-type experiments generate both HCN and acetaldehyde in situ from CH4/NH3/H2/H2O, and alanine forms downstream through Strecker chemistry; the mineral agents here represent subsequent collection and concentration in realistic surface settings rather than the spark itself (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark discharge enhancement of HCN and acetaldehyde supply

conditions.other: spark discharge with H2S-containing volcanic atmosphere; aqueous trapping of products

reactions.mechanism: Sulfur-bearing spark chemistry increases the inventory of aldehydes, nitriles, and amino acid precursors relative to the classic Miller setup.
reactions.reasoning: H2S-containing Miller apparatuses gave amino acid inventories richer than the classic experiment and enhanced alanine formation; this reaction supplies the same convergent hubs HCN and acetaldehyde under sulfur-rich surface conditions (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox homologation to acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)-Cu(II) photoredox system with phosphate present
conditions.other: UV irradiation, neutral to near-neutral pH

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes reductive homologation under H2S/UV photoredox cycling to furnish aldehyde intermediates including acetaldehyde precursors feeding amino acid synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanosulfidic systems chemistry produces aldehyde precursors and integrates amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid precursor synthesis; acetaldehyde formation from HCN-derived chemistry is central to the Patel and Ritson-Sutherland framework (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Formaldehyde conversion to acetaldehyde through surface photochemical carbonyl chemistry

conditions.catalyst: UV-active mineral surfaces with borate-stabilized carbonyl pools
conditions.other: UV-exposed shallow pools; network-level abstraction of photochemical carbonyl buildup from formaldehyde solutions

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical and carbonyl-condensation chemistry in concentrated surface pools channels formaldehyde toward higher aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies UV photolysis of formaldehyde solutions as one of the documented prebiotic routes to acetaldehyde; this reaction is included as a surface bridge from hydrothermal one-carbon products to Strecker chemistry, while acknowledging it as a network-level condensation rather than a single elementary transformation (summary in the provided review citing prebiotic acetaldehyde sources; Pinto et al. discussed formaldehyde abundance).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN in concentrated eutectic or evaporitic settings

conditions.other: HCN concentration above about 0.01 M; eutectic freezing near 250K or evaporative concentration

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN polymerizes through dimeric and trimeric nitrile intermediates to the tetramer diaminomaleonitrile, a major gateway into HCN polymer chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the canonical HCN tetramer and a major branch point in prebiotic nitrile networks, accessible only after strong concentration such as eutectic freezing or evaporation; this concentration requirement is explicitly highlighted in the literature (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa, Cleaves, and Miller, 2002).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; imine formation followed by cyanide addition; acidic or basic pH feasible

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine, followed by cyanide addition to yield alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic Strecker route established for alanine chemistry: acetaldehyde + NH3 + HCN gives alpha-aminopropionitrile, the key aminonitrile precursor to alanine (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: UV irradiation, near-neutral pH, phosphoro-Strecker conditions; reported about 70% yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Under UV-driven cyanosulfidic photoredox conditions, acetaldehyde is converted to alpha-aminopropionitrile in a phosphate-buffered nitrile chemistry network.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. specifically reported alpha-aminopropionitrile formation from acetaldehyde at roughly 70% yield under cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker conditions, making this one of the strongest literature-supported alanine-precursor routes (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide

conditions.other: sequential nitrile hydrolysis under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Partial hydrolysis of the nitrile yields the corresponding amide alaninamide before full conversion to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review explicitly identifies alaninamide as the sequential hydrolysis intermediate between alpha-aminopropionitrile and alanine in Strecker synthesis; representing it separately resolves the prior overcompression issue (Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis of amide to amino acid

reactions.mechanism: Further hydrolysis of alaninamide furnishes racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis from aminonitrile to amide to amino acid is the established Strecker mechanism for alanine formation and avoids collapsing several mechanistic steps into one unsupported transformation (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S to FeS2 redox coupling on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal conditions; oxidative pyrite formation provides reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate condenses with ammonia to an imine/iminium equivalent that is reduced by iron-sulfur redox chemistry to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. demonstrated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine coupled to oxidative pyrite formation, making this a core hydrothermal alanine route (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: mixed Fe(II)/Fe(III) mineral assemblages, optimal near 1:1 ratio
conditions.other: alkaline conditions around pH 9, vent-sediment compatible

reactions.mechanism: Mixed-valence iron minerals mediate electron transfer needed for reductive amination of pyruvate by ammonia to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed pyruvate-to-alanine conversion driven by mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides under alkaline conditions, with maximal yield near a 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio; magnetite-rich vent sediments are a plausible mineral analog in network form (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni-catalyzed H2-driven biomimetic reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: Ni catalyst in anoxic aqueous solution
conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; room-temperature biomimetic reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: Nickel catalyzes hydrogenation of the pyruvate-derived imine/iminium in the presence of ammonium, forming alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur, Rauscher, and Werner showed that nickel catalysts use H2 gas at 5 bar to drive reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine at pH 7-8 and 22 degrees C; iron-sulfur minerals are included as the hydrothermal mineral context required by the schema while the literature-supported Ni catalyst is specified in conditions (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of pyruvate-derived aminonitrile as 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type hydantoin chemistry; CO2 capture of the aminonitrile-derived intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia, and the resulting intermediate captures CO2 to form the hydantoin ring, 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. showed that pyruvate, cyanide, and ammonia can proceed through a hydantoin branch where decarboxylation-released CO2 is recaptured to form 5-methylhydantoin rather than direct aminonitrile hydrolysis; separating this step addresses the prior overcompression critique (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis at about 80C

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin gives N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs pathway proceeds through 5-methylhydantoin and then N-carbamoyl alanine before alanine, so this intermediate must be represented explicitly for chemical fidelity (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.other: hydrolysis at about 80C

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic de-carbamoylation yields racemic alanine from the carbamoyl amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Warm hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine is the final documented stage of the Bucherer-Bergs route to alanine in the recent prebiotic study (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis to racemic amino acids including alanine

conditions.other: alkaline polymerization followed by acid or aqueous hydrolysis of complex HCN-derived material

reactions.mechanism: Complex HCN oligomers and polymers derived from DAMN-like material are hydrolyzed to mixed amino acid products that include alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine formation after hydrolysis of black HCN polymeric material, not as a clean DAMN-to-alanine elementary step; this reaction explicitly treats the process as hydrolysis of complex oligomeric HCN material to avoid the earlier overcompressed representation (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: UV photolysis of interstellar ice analog feedstocks to DL-alanine

conditions.temperature: 12K photolysis followed by warming to 300K for recovery
conditions.pressure: low during ice phase, then 1 atm after deposition
conditions.other: interstellar or cometary ice chemistry followed by delivery and release; alanine about 5% relative to glycine

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of mixed ices generates amino acids in the residue upon warm-up and hydrolysis, including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Bernstein et al. and Muñoz Caro et al. reported alanine production from UV-processed interstellar ice analogs containing H2O, CH3OH, NH3, and HCN; the network treats subsequent delivery to surface environments as the connection step (Bernstein et al., 2002; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type amino acid synthesis yielding alanine

conditions.other: 80-120C, about 10 days, anoxic vent-like conditions

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal carbonyl/sulfide chemistry on Ni,Fe sulfides converts CO-, cyanide-, and thiol-containing feedstocks into a suite of amino acids including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wächtershäuser reported alpha-amino acids including alanine under hydrothermal Ni,Fe sulfide conditions from CO/KCN/CH3SH feedstocks; this replaces the previously criticized oversimplified direct FTT alanine step with the actual literature-supported substrate set (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: CISS-enabled emergence of homochiral proto-RNA from surface photogeochemistry

conditions.catalyst: magnetite photoelectrons and mineral surfaces in shallow lakes
conditions.other: UV irradiation; symmetry breaking via chiral-induced spin selectivity; network-level precursor to stereoselective biopolymer function

reactions.mechanism: Spin-polarized photoelectrons generated on magnetite under UV irradiation bias precursor crystallization and assembly toward homochiral informational polymers.
reactions.reasoning: The literature argues that alanine homochirality is more plausibly downstream of earlier homochiral RNA-like systems than the result of direct alanine enantioselection. Ozturk and Sasselov proposed CISS-driven enantioselective photochemistry on magnetite, and Ozturk et al. experimentally demonstrated homochiral crystallization of ribo-aminooxazoline, motivating a systems-level homochiral proto-RNA node (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk, Liu, Sutherland, and Sasselov, 2023; Ozturk et al., 2025).

reactions.name: Biochemical stereoselective recruitment of L-alanine from racemic alanine by homochiral proto-RNA/peptide network

conditions.catalyst: template-directed stereoselective selection in protocellular or mineral-interface systems
conditions.other: network-level amplification and selection, not a single established elementary reaction; repeated geothermal and compartmental cycling

reactions.mechanism: A pre-existing homochiral informational/polymeric system selectively incorporates or enriches the L-enantiomer from racemic alanine pools, coupling symmetry breaking to biochemical function.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature explicitly rejects a simple direct DL-to-L alanine synthesis step and instead points to indirect emergence of L-alanine through homochiral RNA-mediated or autocatalytic network selection. This reaction is therefore intentionally framed as a systems-level selection/amplification module rather than a mechanistically complete elementary reaction, consistent with current open questions (Blackmond et al., 2025; Ozturk et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic spark-Strecker alanine pathway
pathways.description: Reduced atmospheric spark discharge supplies HCN and acetaldehyde, which undergo classical Strecker chemistry and sequential hydrolysis to racemic alanine before later biochemical L-selection.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S spark-Strecker pathway
pathways.description: A sulfur-rich volcanic Miller-Urey variant enhances nitrile and aldehyde production, then proceeds through Strecker aminonitrile and hydrolysis chemistry.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker pathway
pathways.description: HCN is photoreduced in sulfur- and phosphate-bearing surface pools to acetaldehyde and then to alpha-aminopropionitrile under Patel-Sutherland conditions, followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to surface acetaldehyde Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies formaldehyde, which is transferred to surface photochemical pools and converted to acetaldehyde, then onward through Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Pyrite-coupled hydrothermal reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal protometabolic carbon fixation generates pyruvate, which undergoes pyrite-coupled reductive amination to racemic alanine.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron mineral reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is aminated by ammonia on mixed Fe(II)/Fe(III) mineral assemblages under alkaline vent-compatible conditions.

pathways.name: Ni-H2 biomimetic pyruvate amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermally available pyruvate is reduced and aminated by H2 over Ni under mild aqueous conditions closely paralleling modern biosynthesis.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form 5-methylhydantoin, which hydrolyzes through N-carbamoyl alanine to racemic alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis pathway
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN forms DAMN and related polymers, whose hydrolysis yields mixed amino acids including alanine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal NiFeS amino acid synthesis pathway
pathways.description: Under hydrothermal Ni,Fe sulfide catalysis, CO/KCN/CH3SH feedstocks yield amino acids including alanine directly, feeding the later selection stage.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The classic spark-Strecker sequence to DL-alanine is strongly literature-supported and chemically coherent. Miller-type discharge plausibly generates HCN and acetaldehyde, and Strecker hydrolysis to racemic alanine is one of the best-established alanine routes. The weakness is the final DL-alanine to L-alanine conversion, which is not an established single reaction but a speculative systems-level selection module. Thus the route is excellent to DL-alanine but only moderately convincing for the stated target L-alanine.
suggestions: Recast the pathway endpoint as DL-alanine if strict chemical target validation is required.
If retaining L-alanine, describe rxn_022 as network-level enrichment rather than direct conversion.
Add explicit magnetite/CISS linkage and amplification mechanism details for the chirality-selection module.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the best-supported surface routes. The H2S-enhanced volcanic Miller variant is directly aligned with Parker et al. and the deep research summary that alanine yield increases markedly in sulfur-rich spark experiments. Strecker chemistry remains robust. As with P1, the principal weakness is not alanine formation but unsupported direct realization of enantiopure L-alanine.
suggestions: Preserve the strong literature grounding by reporting a racemic intermediate endpoint separately.
Incorporate an explicit statement that chirality emergence is unresolved and not a discrete reaction step.
Specify the actual mineral/photochemical agent set for the chirality module if it is to remain.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The phosphoro-Strecker branch is highly aligned with Patel et al. and is one of the strongest routes to alpha-aminopropionitrile. The route from HCN/H2S to acetaldehyde is plausible in the broader cyanosulfidic framework, though represented here somewhat generically and with less mechanistic precision than the aminonitrile-forming step. Overall this is the best-supported route to alanine precursor chemistry, but the final L-selection step remains speculative.
suggestions: Clarify rxn_005 with Cu photoredox mediation and explicit homologation intermediates.
Differentiate clearly between experimentally validated racemic synthesis and hypothetical chiral enrichment.
State neutral pH and ~35 C equivalent conditions more explicitly in the pathway description.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The hydrothermal-to-surface handoff is conceptually valuable, but the chemistry is weaker than other routes. Hydrothermal formaldehyde production from CO2/H2 on Fe(Ni)S has support, yet direct conversion of formaldehyde to acetaldehyde as a standalone surface photochemical step is underspecified and less established than routes via glycolaldehyde or cyanosulfidic homologation. The downstream Strecker sequence is solid, but this pathway's bottleneck is acetaldehyde generation.
suggestions: Replace direct formaldehyde-to-acetaldehyde with a better-supported sequence through glycolaldehyde.
Add borate or other explicit surface agents if sugar/carbonyl homologation is intended.
Retain this as a supporting interconnection pathway rather than a flagship synthesis route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate is very strongly supported by Hafenbradl et al. and fits the iron-sulfur world framework well. The weaker point is hydrothermal pyruvate generation from CO2, which is plausible but depends strongly on the exact catalyst network and conditions. This gives a good route to DL-alanine, but again not a validated direct route to L-alanine.
suggestions: Specify pyrite-forming FeS/H2S coupling conditions more explicitly in the pyruvate amination step.
Strengthen the pyruvate source with a more explicit reverse-TCA or Fe/Ni nanoparticle context.
Treat chiral outcome as post-synthetic enrichment rather than final reaction conversion.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway aligns well with Barge et al. on mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide-mediated pyruvate amination under alkaline conditions. It is chemically plausible and metabolically relevant. The limitations are the upstream pyruvate synthesis uncertainty and the unresolved final enantioenrichment module.
suggestions: Include the optimal mixed Fe(II)/Fe(III) ratio and alkaline vent context in the pathway description.
Clarify whether the hydrothermal environment here is warm alkaline vent rather than high-temperature black smoker.
Separate racemic alanine production from chirality-selection network steps.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Ni/H2 biomimetic pyruvate amination step is one of the most modern and compelling literature-supported reactions, closely matching Kaur et al. The route is chemically strong for DL-alanine production under mild, plausible conditions. The remaining issue is that the target is L-alanine, and the final stereoselective recruitment step remains hypothetical rather than experimentally established.
suggestions: State the 5 bar H2, pH 7-8, and room-temperature conditions explicitly if not already encoded.
Avoid overstating this as hydrothermal if the cited experiment is closer to mild aqueous geochemistry.
Use this as a high-confidence route to racemic alanine feeding a separate chirality module.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is well aligned with the recent Bucherer-Bergs revival literature and nicely captures the pyruvate branch point highlighted in the review. Formation of 5-methylhydantoin and hydrolysis through N-carbamoyl alanine is coherent and experimentally grounded. As elsewhere, the only serious weakness is the leap from racemic alanine chemistry to L-alanine as target.
suggestions: Include the 80 C hydrolysis context and explicit CO2 capture rationale in the step annotations.
Emphasize the link between amino acid and nucleobase chemistry as a network strength.
Represent the final L-alanine step as emergent selection rather than direct product conversion.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is well known, and hydrolysis of HCN polymers can indeed yield alanine among mixed amino acid products. However, this is a diffuse, low-selectivity route rather than a clean directed pathway, so coherence and target specificity are weaker than for Strecker or pyruvate amination. It contributes useful convergence but is not a strong principal route to L-alanine.
suggestions: Treat this as a background supply route to racemic amino acid mixtures rather than direct alanine synthesis.
Add concentration context such as eutectic freezing to justify efficient HCN oligomerization.
Do not rely on this pathway for target-specific achievement.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type amino acid synthesis over NiFeS has literature support, but it is product-diverse and less specifically resolved than Strecker or pyruvate amination. As a short route it helps network completeness, but it is less mechanistically transparent and less tightly integrated with the other hubs. The speculative chirality module again limits target achievement.
suggestions: Clarify whether alanine is produced directly or among a mixture of amino acids under these conditions.
Tie this route more explicitly to the stated hydrothermal mineral system and temperature window.
Use it as a supporting auxiliary source rather than a core pathway.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a strong and well-connected synthesis network for prebiotic alanine chemistry and a reasonably sophisticated attempt to address the far harder target of L-alanine. Topologically, the network is good: it has ample molecule and reaction nodes, multiple genuine hubs (HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, alpha-aminopropionitrile, DL-alanine), and several convergent routes centered on literature-established branch points. Environmental interconnection is also a major strength, especially the handoff from hydrothermal carbon fixation and pyruvate chemistry into surface Strecker/cyanosulfidic chemistry and finally into a biochemical selection module. The network is broadly consistent with the review: it captures the major experimentally supported alanine-forming routes including Miller-Urey/spark discharge, H2S-enhanced spark chemistry, Strecker synthesis, pyruvate reductive amination, Bucherer-Bergs chemistry, HCN polymer hydrolysis, UV ice analog production, and hydrothermal NiFeS synthesis. It also correctly reflects the literature consensus that direct prebiotic routes yield racemic alanine and that L-alanine is an unresolved systems-level problem. The main weaknesses are threefold. First, some individual reactions are oversimplified relative to the literature, especially direct formaldehyde-to-acetaldehyde conversion and the generic cyanosulfidic acetaldehyde-forming step. Second, not all hydrothermal/surface steps visibly satisfy the strict mineral-agent specification requirement in the summarized network presentation, though the chemistry often implies them. Third and most importantly, the final conversion from DL-alanine to L-alanine is not a chemically established discrete reaction; it is a plausible but still speculative network-level symmetry-breaking/amplification construct. Because the target is specifically L-alanine rather than alanine/DL-alanine, this prevents a fully confident approval despite excellent literature alignment for the racemic synthesis core.
